examples.title: 1D Array Declaration and Access
examples.description: Demonstrates static array declaration, initialization, and element access in C
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates static array declaration with size 5, initialization with values, accessing elements using indices (0-based), and calculating memory addresses using the formula: address = base + (index × element_size).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for traversal, O(1) for access by index
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n=5 for static array

examples.title: Dynamic Array Implementation
examples.description: Python list demonstrating dynamic array behavior with automatic resizing
examples.code:
# Python lists are dynamic arrays
arr = []

# Appending elements (automatic resizing)
arr.append(10)
arr.append(20)
arr.append(30)
arr.append(40)
arr.append(50)

print("Array after appending:", arr)
print("Length:", len(arr))
print("Element at index 2:", arr[2])

# Insert at beginning - O(n)
arr.insert(0, 5)
print("After inserting 5 at index 0:", arr)

# Insert at end - O(1) amortized
arr.append(60)
print("After appending 60:", arr)

# Check current capacity (implementation detail)
import sys
print("Memory size:", sys.getsizeof(arr), "bytes")
examples.explanation: Python lists are implemented as dynamic arrays that automatically resize. When the internal array fills up, Python allocates a new larger array (typically 1.125× or 2× the size) and copies elements. This provides amortized O(1) time for append operations.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) amortized for append, O(n) for insert at beginning

examples.title: 2D Array Row-Major Implementation
examples.description: C program demonstrating 2D array storage in row-major order with address calculation
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates 2D array storage in row-major order. The formula Address = Base + ((row × number_of_columns) + column) × element_size calculates any element's memory location. For matrix[2][3] in a 3×4 matrix stored at base 1000 with 4-byte integers: index = (2×4) + 3 = 11, address = 1000 + 44 = 1044.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for address calculation, O(n×m) for full matrix traversal

examples.title: Polynomial Addition Using Arrays
examples.description: Algorithm to add two polynomials represented as arrays where index represents exponent
examples.explanation: This code represents polynomials using arrays where the index represents the exponent and the value represents the coefficient. To add two polynomials, we simply add coefficients at each index (exponent). The time complexity is O(n) where n is the maximum degree of the polynomials.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the maximum degree
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing the result polynomial
